blocks.statement: I can describe a heat engine cycle (heat in from hot reservoir → some work out → waste heat to cold reservoir), compute efficiency from Q_hot and W, and compare to the Carnot limit η_max = 1 − T_cold/T_hot.

blocks.whatWeKnow: The expanding fireball after impact is a NATURAL heat engine — it converts some thermal energy to mechanical work (the shockwave).
blocks.connection: No engine — not even an impact fireball — converts 100% of heat to work. The second law forbids it. Day 11's KE partition (vaporization + shockwave + thermal radiation) reflects exactly this limit.

blocks.markdown:
## Heat in, work out, waste required

A **heat engine** is any device that turns heat into work: car engine, power plant, jet turbine — or an impact fireball. Every one of them runs the same three-arrow cycle:

1. **Q_hot** flows IN from a hot reservoir (burning gasoline, steam, the fireball).
2. SOME of it leaves as useful **work W** (piston, turbine, shockwave).
3. The REST is dumped as waste heat **Q_cold** to a cold reservoir (exhaust, cooling tower, the surrounding air).

Energy conservation ties them together: **Q_hot = W + Q_cold**. The fraction that becomes work is the **efficiency**:

**η = W / Q_hot = 1 − Q_cold/Q_hot** — always less than 1.

And the second law puts a hard ceiling on it. The best ANY engine can do between a hot reservoir at T_H and a cold one at T_C (both in Kelvin) is the **Carnot limit**:

**η_max = 1 − T_C/T_H**

No design, no fuel, no clever engineering beats it. A real engine can only creep closer.

terms.definition: A device that takes heat IN from a hot reservoir, converts SOME to useful work, and DUMPS the rest as waste heat to a cold reservoir. Car engine: gasoline combustion (hot) → piston work (W) → exhaust heat (Q_cold to outside). Power plant: steam (hot) → turbine work → cooling tower (cold). The cold reservoir is REQUIRED — without a cold side the engine can't work; the second law mandates waste heat.
terms.cognate: Sp. máquina térmica · Pt. máquina térmica · HC machin a chalè

terms.definition: The fraction of heat input that becomes useful work. η = W / Q_hot = 1 − Q_cold/Q_hot. Always < 1. Power plant: 1000 J in, 400 J of work, 600 J waste → η = 400/1000 = 40%. Car: ~25%. Human body: ~25%. η is dimensionless (between 0 and 1, or 0% and 100%); 1 (100%) is impossible.
terms.cognate: Sp. eficiencia · Pt. eficiência · HC efikasite

terms.definition: The MAXIMUM possible efficiency for an engine between hot reservoir at T_H (Kelvin) and cold reservoir at T_C (Kelvin). η_max = 1 − T_C/T_H. Real engines are always below this. Power plant with T_H = 600 K, T_C = 300 K → η_max = 1 − 300/600 = 0.50 = 50%. Real plant gets 40% — below the limit.
terms.cognate: Sp. límite de Carnot · Pt. limite de Carnot

blocks.title: USE KELVIN. ALWAYS.
blocks.markdown: T_C/T_H is a RATIO — only Kelvin gives the right ratio. Don't use °C. (300 K / 600 K = 0.5, but 27 °C / 327 °C = 0.08 — nonsense.) Convert first: K = °C + 273.15.

links.label: Q_hot in
links.sublabel: 1000 J from hot reservoir (600 K steam)

links.label: Q_cold dumped
links.sublabel: 600 J waste heat to cold reservoir (300 K cooling tower)

blocks.instruction: Draw the heat-engine box diagram: hot reservoir on top, engine box in the middle, cold reservoir on the bottom. Label Q_hot, W, and Q_cold with arrows.
prompts: Label the power-plant numbers: 1000 J in, 400 J of work out — where does the other 600 J go?
Compute the efficiency for that example and write it inside the engine box
Compute the Carnot limit at T_H = 500 K, T_C = 300 K and compare

blocks.prompt: POWER PLANT EFFICIENCY — A power plant takes 1000 J of heat from steam at 600 K, produces 400 J of useful work, and dumps 600 J as waste heat to a 300-K cooling tower. (a) Real efficiency? (b) Carnot limit at these temperatures? (c) Is the real engine doing OK?
blocks.answer: 40% efficient. Carnot limit is 50%. The plant is at 80% of the theoretical max.

blocks.prompt: CAR ENGINE — A car engine receives 1500 J of heat per cycle and produces 400 J of work. (a) Efficiency? (b) How much heat is wasted to exhaust?
blocks.givenHint: Q_hot = 1500 J · W = 400 J per cycle
blocks.equationHint: η = W/Q_hot for part (a). For part (b): Q_cold = Q_hot − W.

blocks.prompt: CARNOT FOR A STEAM ENGINE — A steam engine runs between T_H = 450 K (steam) and T_C = 300 K (condenser). What is the maximum possible efficiency?
blocks.givenHint: T_H = 450 K · T_C = 300 K (already in Kelvin — no conversion needed)
blocks.equationHint: η_max = 1 − T_C/T_H — temperatures in Kelvin only.

blocks.title: The Carnot limit is set by the TEMPERATURES alone
blocks.markdown: Not by the working fluid or the engine design. You can never beat it. You can only get closer to it.

blocks.prompt: A car engine receives 1500 J of heat per cycle and produces 400 J of work. What's its efficiency? Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: An engine's efficiency is bounded by η_max = ___, which depends only on the hot and cold ___ (in ___). Real engines fall ___ this limit because of friction and other losses.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **24.5** (heat engines and the second law — Carnot's ideal efficiency). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
